Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-03-24#

Run ID: run-2026-03-24-648b963111 · Status: success · Source data: 2026-03-23 · Generated: 2026-03-24T16:25:46.621Z

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 27 days of data (2026-02-26 to 2026-03-24), government website accessibility scores have improved (+23.5 points, from 68 to 91.5). Performance scores have improved (+16.37 points, from 38 to 54.37).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 92.33 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 100 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

image-alt: Visual Content Access

Images without alternative text are completely invisible to blind users and to search engines. On government websites, informational images, diagrams, infographics, and document screenshots without alt text deprive blind citizens of the same information available to sighted visitors. Critical details like emergency instructions, benefit eligibility charts, or form procedure diagrams can be entirely inaccessible, putting these citizens at a disadvantage in accessing government services and information.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools

link-in-text-block: Color-Independent Link Identification

When links are distinguished only by color, people with color vision deficiency cannot identify them as links and may miss critical references to additional information, forms, or resources. On government websites, overlooked links in informational content can mean missing essential instructions, deadlines, or procedures that affect a citizen's ability to access services or comply with requirements.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision who may not perceive color differences
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

listitem: List Item Semantic Context

Orphaned list items outside of list containers lose their semantic meaning and list navigation properties. Screen reader users rely on list structure to understand that a set of items is related and to count the total number of items. Government navigation menus, step lists, and option groups presented as invalid lists are harder to parse and navigate efficiently.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard

html-has-lang: Language Identification

Without a language attribute, screen readers default to the user's system language settings, which may cause government content to be read in the wrong language with incorrect pronunciation. This is particularly harmful for multilingual citizens accessing government services in their non-primary language, and for users of braille displays that depend on language metadata for proper rendering.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with cognitive or language differences
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

NN/g usability heuristics affected by today's top accessibility violations
# Heuristic Issue patterns URL violations Related axe rules
6 Recognition rather than recall 5 84 heading-order, landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-name, listitem
4 Consistency and standards 5 80 heading-order, landmark-one-main, label-content-name-mismatch, link-name, listitem
8 Aesthetic and minimalist design 5 67 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, image-alt, link-in-text-block, listitem
10 Help and documentation 5 67 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, image-alt, link-in-text-block, listitem
1 Visibility of system status 4 56 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-in-text-block, listitem
9 Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors 4 56 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-in-text-block, listitem
7 Flexibility and efficiency of use 3 51 heading-order, label-content-name-mismatch, link-name
5 Error prevention 3 38 landmark-one-main, link-in-text-block, listitem

Americans Excluded by Disability Today#

Based on 70,669,076 page loads across successfully scanned government URLs and U.S. disability prevalence rates from the U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101 (2023), an estimated 4,960,000 Americans encountered an accessibility barrier on a government website today.

Each row shows the number of people in a disability group who visited a page with at least one accessibility issue that affects their group. Prevalence rates and U.S. population estimates are derived from the American Community Survey (ACS) and supplemental sources. These figures are rough estimates intended to illustrate the scale of accessibility barriers.

Estimated Americans excluded today by disability category (2023 Census data)
Icon Disability Group (Section 508 FPC) U.S. Prevalence U.S. Population Affected Page Loads with Barriers Est. Americans Excluded Today
Limited Reach and Strength 5.8% ~19,600,000 30,700,000 1,780,000
Limited Manipulation 2.2% ~7,600,000 45,900,000 1,010,000
Without Perception of Color 4.3% ~14,500,000 17,700,000 760,000
Limited Vision 2.4% ~8,100,000 23,800,000 570,000
Without Vision 1.0% ~3,400,000 45,500,000 450,000
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities 4.7% ~15,900,000 5,220,000 240,000
Without Hearing 0.3% ~1,100,000 45,500,000 130,000

Sources: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; disability prevalence from ACS 2023 and supplemental CDC/NIDCD/NIH data. Reviewed annually.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 96 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on March 24, 2026
Metric Estimated total (March 24, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 9 years, 5 months, 18 days 298,764,382 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~55.3 TB (2,301 copies of Wikipedia) Across 96 URLs with weight data

Extra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Extra data is calculated as: max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic.

Observed USWDS versions: 2.9.0Sites: fbi.gov, 3.8.0Sites: fdic.gov. The latest release is available at github.com/uswds/uswds/releases.

Section 508: Legal Requirements vs. Best Practices#

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act was enacted in 1998 and requires U.S. federal agencies to make their electronic and information technology accessible to people with disabilities. The technical standards were significantly updated through the ICT Accessibility Standards refresh, which became effective in January 2018 — the same year Apple celebrated the 10th anniversary of the iPhone. That refresh incorporated WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published in 2008) as the baseline for web content.

The web has changed dramatically since then. What agencies are legally required to meet today is built on a standard from 2008 — the same era as the original iPhone. Agencies should treat WCAG 2.0 AA as the floor, not the ceiling.

Legal Requirement

WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published 2008)

Referenced by the Section 508 ICT Standards refresh (effective 2018). Agencies must meet these 38 success criteria to satisfy their Section 508 obligations for web content.

  • Text alternatives for non-text content
  • Captions and audio descriptions for multimedia
  • Keyboard accessibility and focus management
  • Minimum color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text)
  • No seizure-inducing content
  • Consistent navigation and labeling
  • Error identification and correction

Section 508 web content requirements (E205)

Best Practices

WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 Level AA (published 2018 and 2023)

These later versions add criteria that reflect how the web actually works today — mobile devices, cognitive accessibility, and modern authentication patterns — and are strongly recommended for any agency aiming to serve all Americans. The US Federal Government is one of the last to use the legacy WCAG 2.0 AA Standard, as it was developed ~2 decades ago.

  • WCAG 2.1 (2018): 17 new criteria for mobile, low vision, and cognitive accessibility
  • WCAG 2.2 (2023): 9 more criteria for authentication and cognitive tasks, removing the outdated "Parsing" criterion
  • W3C now recommends WCAG 2.2 as the current normative standard for new and updated content

What is new in WCAG 2.2 · WCAG 2.2 specification

The scores and findings in this report are based on automated checks aligned with WCAG 2.x rules surfaced by axe-core. Passing automated checks is necessary but not sufficient: manual testing with assistive technologies is required to verify true conformance and to catch barriers that automation cannot detect.

Take Action#

Today's scan identified 212 accessibility barriers across the most-visited U.S. government websites, affecting an estimated 4,960,000 Americans with disabilities. Here is how you can help.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~123KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~123,681 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 12,368,133 affected page loads) ~37KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~37,104 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 12,368,133 affected page loads) ~272KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~272,099 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 12,368,133 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 2

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 3

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

<ul class="nav-list" role="menubar">

Label: Skip Quick Tools Links Quick Tools Track a Package Informed Delivery …

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not have valid values (rule: aria-valid-attr-value)

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid values. Learn more about valid values for ARIA attributes.

Element 1

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Invalid ARIA attribute value: aria-controls="collapseOne"

Element 2

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Invalid ARIA attribute value: aria-controls="collapseOne"

Element 3

Element path: div.col-sm-12 > div#accordion > div.panel > div.panel-heading

Snippet:

<div class="panel-heading collapser" role="tab" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseone">

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://forecast.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 67

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~42KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~42,565 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,773,558 affected page loads) ~76KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~76,263 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 1,773,558 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~17KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~17,736 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,773,558 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~83,357 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 1,773,558 affected page loads)

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16.4px instead of at least 24px.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~12KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~12,791 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,279,069 affected page loads) ~3KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1

Element path: div > div.blazy > div.media > img.media__element

Snippet:

<img decoding="async" class="media__element b-lazy" loading="lazy" src="" width="480" height="360">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

https://travel.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~24KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~24,328 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,013,663 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Snippet:

<h4 _ngcontent-thb-c154="" id="fsa_Title_PopularTasks" class="text-left fsa-mb-px-16 fsa-color-white">

Label: POPULAR TOPICS

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~21KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~21,582 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 899,232 affected page loads) ~38KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~38,667 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 899,232 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/announcements-events/idr-court-actions">

Label: visit StudentAid.gov/courtactions

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element 2

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/fsa-id/sign-in/landing/?redirectTo=/my-aid">

Label: Log in

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~19KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~19,783 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 899,232 affected page loads) ~52KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~52,155 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 899,232 affected page loads)

Label: Site Survey

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (61px by 23px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 23px instead of at least 24px.

https://cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 67

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~17KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~17,137 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 714,035 affected page loads) ~30KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~30,704 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 714,035 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~7KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~7,140 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 714,035 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,142 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 714,035 affected page loads) ~33KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~33,560 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 714,035 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~7KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~7,140 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 714,035 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,142 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 714,035 affected page loads)

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://egov.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 1

The document uses `<meta http-equiv="refresh">` (rule: meta-refresh)

Users do not expect a page to refresh automatically, and doing so will move focus back to the top of the page. This may create a frustrating or confusing experience. Learn more about the refresh meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,693 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 469,252 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,408 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 469,252 affected page loads) ~10KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~10,324 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 469,252 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="360">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • <meta> tag forces timed refresh of page (less than 20 hours)

Element path: html

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark
`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,925 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 392,519 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,178 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 392,519 affected page loads)

Label: Monthly Export Scientific Names (any rank, any usage) Common Names 24-Feb-2026 …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Some non-empty data cells do not have table headers

Accessibility Findings

https://dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 4

ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name)

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,877 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 387,738 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,163 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 387,738 affected page loads) ~8KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~8,530 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 387,738 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: nav.usa-nav > div.region > div.footer > div#search-block-wrap-1

Snippet:

<div id="search-block-wrap-1" class="tablet:grid-col-4" role="searchbox">

Label: PRESS ENTER TO SEARCH

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~9KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~9,306 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 387,738 affected page loads) ~16KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~16,673 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 387,738 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.subscribe-inner > form#GD-snippet-form > div.inner > div.form-text

Snippet:

<div class="form-text">

Label: Get direct updates from DHS in your inbox.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 4.12 (foreground color: #edeeee, background color: #2e78a9, font size: 15.6pt (20.8px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,877 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 387,738 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~8,530 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 387,738 affected page loads)

Label: NOTICE: CBP Home Stipend Increase

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://urs.earthdata.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 81

Axe findings: 5

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,588 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 358,755 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,076 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 358,755 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#tophat3Widget > nav#tophat3WidgetNavBar > div.container-fluid > button#tophat3HamburgerButton

Snippet:

<button class="navbar-toggler" type="button" id="tophat3HamburgerButton">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~8,610 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 358,755 affected page loads) ~15KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~15,426 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 358,755 affected page loads)

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.07 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #20ce6f, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://radar.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 77

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,931 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 293,133 affected page loads) ~879People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~879 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 293,133 affected page loads) ~13KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~13,777 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 293,133 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~7KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~7,035 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 293,133 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=0">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://my.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Estimated ~2,827 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 282,668 affected page loads) ~848People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,719 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 271,880 affected page loads) ~816People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element path: div.container > div.row > div.col-xs-12 > ul

Snippet:

<ul _ngcontent-ng-c2940030545="">

Label: About ESTA and The Visa Waiver Program What is the difference between an ESTA …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: div
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

<a class="ver12MainLayout" href="/npspub/pub_cmn_login/index_live.jsp">

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

https://coast.noaa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 2

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

  • Element has no title attribute

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Accessibility Findings

https://fs.usda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~4,723 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 214,698 affected page loads) ~12KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~12,452 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 214,698 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.margin-y-1 > div.fs-site-branding > div.display-flex > a.font-body-md

Snippet:

<a class="font-body-md text-no-underline text-base-darker" href="https://www.usda.gov/" data-kmt="1">

Label: U.S.D.A

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (152.8px by 20.3px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20.4px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2

Element path: div.margin-x-0 > div.tablet:display-flex > div.grid-row > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.fs.usda.gov/about-agency/features" data-kmt="1">

Label: More Featured Stories

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (154.2px by 14.9px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 14.8px instead of at least 24px.

Element 3

Element path: div.usa-footer__logo > div.fs-site-branding > div.display-flex > a.font-body-md

Snippet:

<a class="font-body-md text-no-underline text-base-darker" href="https://www.usda.gov/" data-kmt="1">

Label: U.S.D.A

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (152.8px by 20.3px, should be at least 24px by 24px)

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

https://sam.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr)

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.paragraph__column > div.px-video-container > div#progress-bar > div#progress

Snippet:

<div class="progress" id="progress" aria-label="Video: " style="width: 0%;">

Label: Video:

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a div with no valid role attribute.
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

https://tsaenrollmentbyidemia.tsa.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Axe findings: 4

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,886 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 188,648 affected page loads) ~566People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~566 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 188,648 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.col-12 > div.official-banner > div#accordion > img.ues-usa-img

Snippet:

<img src="/images/us_flag_small.png" class="ues-usa-img">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,528 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 188,648 affected page loads) ~8KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~8,112 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 188,648 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.col-12 > div.official-banner > div#accordion > a#banner-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="banner-toggle" data-toggle="collapse" href="#" data-target="#collapseOne" aria-expanded="true">

Label: Here's how you know.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.01:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0f68b8, surrounding text: #373a3c)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,528 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 188,648 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,886 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 188,648 affected page loads) ~566People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~4,150 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 188,648 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html.no-js

Snippet:

<html class="no-js" lang="en">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark

https://nhlbi.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~4,006 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 182,112 affected page loads) ~10KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~10,562 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 182,112 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: nav.block > ul.menu > li.menu-item > a.ext-icon

Snippet:

<a class="ext-icon" href="#">

Label: (link is external)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (24px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 14px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2

Element path: nav.block > ul.menu > li.menu-item > a.ext

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.hhs.gov/" rel="nofollow noopener noreferrer" class="ext" target="_blank">

Label: Health and Human Services

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (167.9px by 18px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20px instead of at least 24px.

Element 3

Element path: nav.block > ul.menu > li.menu-item > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.nih.gov/" rel="noreferrer">

Label: National Institutes of Health

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (181.1px by 18px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20px instead of at least 24px.

Element 4

Element path: nav.block > ul.menu > li.menu-item > a.ext-icon

Snippet:

<a class="ext-icon" href="#">

Label: (link is external)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (24px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 14px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://mymarketnews.ams.usda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 83

Axe findings: 4

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,806 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads) ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,335 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads) ~542People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~542 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,973 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div.flexslider > div.flex-viewport > ul.slides > li.clone

Snippet:

<li class="clone" aria-hidden="true" style="width: 352px; margin-right: 0px; float: left; display: block;">

Label: Contamination-Free Cotton AMS implements new extraneous matter code for plasti…

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Focusable content should have tabindex="-1" or be removed from the DOM

Element 2

Element path: div.flexslider > div.flex-viewport > ul.slides > li.clone

Snippet:

<li style="width: 352px; margin-right: 0px; float: left; display: block;" class="clone" aria-hidden="true">

Label: My Market News Resources Learn more about My Market News

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Focusable content should have tabindex="-1" or be removed from the DOM
`[role]`s do not have all required `[aria-*]` attributes (rule: aria-required-attr)

Some ARIA roles have required attributes that describe the state of the element to screen readers. Learn more about roles and required attributes.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,806 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads) ~542People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~3,973 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: header#headerTop > div.container > div.row > div.col-sm-12

Snippet:

<div class="col-sm-12" role="heading">

Label: About AMS News and Announcements Careers For Employees Contact Us A-Z Glossary …

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Required ARIA attribute not present: aria-level
Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,806 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads) ~542People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~542 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.form-item > div.input-group > span.input-group-btn > button.button

Snippet:

<button class="button js-form-submit form-submit btn-primary form-control icon-only" name="" type="submit" value="Search">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,335 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads) ~7KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~7,766 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 180,607 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: section#block-aboutmarketnews > div.field > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="/Public%20Notices">

Label: view

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.51:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #337ab7, surrounding text: #5a5a5a)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Accessibility Findings

https://faa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,197 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 133,216 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,728 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 133,216 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: form > div.row > div.home__form > input.btn

Snippet:

<input type="submit" value="Submit" class="btn btn-gold my-0">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.33 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #b0862c, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

Element path: div.quick_search-delays > form#faa-airport-status-lookup-form > div.col-4 > input#airport-code-status-search-submit

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.33 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #b0862c, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,332 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 133,216 affected page loads) ~400People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,931 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 133,216 affected page loads)

Element path: form > div.row > div.col-12 > span.tool

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has a tabindex greater than 0

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,289 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 128,869 affected page loads) ~387People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Tables do not use `<caption>` instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute to indicate a caption. (rule: table-fake-caption)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that tables use the actual caption element instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about captions.

Accessibility Findings

https://ipps-a.army.mil

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 83

Axe findings: 5

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,255 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 125,507 affected page loads) ~377People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. <a id="skip-target" name="skip-target" class="skip" tabindex="-1" innertext="Start of main content" role="button">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,201 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 120,123 affected page loads) ~360People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Label: CONTACT SUPPORT

  • Heading order invalid
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,883 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 120,123 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,165 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 120,123 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.col-12 > div.row > div.col-12 > a.enrollment-link

Snippet:

<a data-bs-target="#mdl-what-i-need" data-bs-toggle="modal" href="" translate="" class="enrollment-link">

Label: What Information do I need?

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #00568f, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://aviationweather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 92

Axe findings: 3

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~2,719 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 113,307 affected page loads) ~4KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~4,872 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 113,307 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.container > div.carousel-item > div.d-block > h4

Snippet:

<h4>

Label: Welcome!

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 4.44 (foreground color: #f8f9fa, background color: #6c757d, font size: 16.2pt (21.636px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,133 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 113,307 affected page loads) ~340People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~340 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 113,307 affected page loads) ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,493 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 113,307 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.card > div.card-body > a.btn > h4

Snippet:

<h4>

Label: Expanded Archive

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,133 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 113,307 affected page loads) ~340People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Accessibility Findings

https://noaa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,477 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 112,612 affected page loads) ~6KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~6,531 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 112,612 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div#footer-menus > ul#menu--footer-2 > li.menu__item > a.menu__link

Snippet:

<a href="/protecting-your-privacy" class="menu__link" data-ga="Footer:Protecting Your Privacy" data-drupal-link-system-path="node/5778">

Label: Protecting Your Privacy

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (158.8px by 22px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 22px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2

Element path: div#footer-menus > ul#menu--footer-2 > li.menu__item > a.menu__link

Snippet:

<a href="https://vote.gov/" class="menu__link" data-ga="Footer:Vote.gov">

Label: Vote.gov

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (60.4px by 22px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 22px instead of at least 24px.
